proofBoundary: Export-proven workflow canvas layout guidance derived from user-provided workflow JSON references and screenshots. This reference validates node placement, workflow action layout, outcome SequenceFlow shape, gateway placement, rejection endpoint grouping, and line routing readability only; it does not validate assignee correctness, workflow execution, or runtime publishing.

sourceReferences.description: Complex approval workflow with horizontal approval backbone, inclusive branch gateways, lower-lane cancel/reject handling, and explicit vertex routing on many long transitions.
layoutPatterns: Use a readable horizontal backbone for the main happy path.
Place reject/cancel paths on separate vertical lanes instead of stacking them over task nodes.
Use vertices for long return, reject, merge, and cross-lane transitions.
Spread branch gateways and approval tasks across columns instead of reusing one coordinate.

sourceReferences.description: Large reimbursement workflow with multiple vertical lanes, recurring approval/action blocks, branch fan-out/fan-in, and rounded/vertex-routed sequence flows.
layoutPatterns: Use multiple vertical lanes for side branches, optional reviewers, finance handling, and final archival actions.
Use rounded sequence flows for complex routes.
Add vertices to long cross-lane links so lines do not cut through unrelated nodes.
Keep Start/End/reject outcomes visually separated from task/action clusters.

sourceReferences.description: Focused workflow action layout reference with Start, Approval Assignment Tasks, Complete Assignment Tasks, Inclusive Gateway amount branches, shared End with Rejection nodes, and one rejected return line using explicit vertices.
layoutPatterns: Use a fixed main lane around y = 40 for the approval backbone.
Use about 305-335 px horizontal spacing between Start, Assignment Tasks, Gateways, and End nodes.
Use Inclusive Gateway nodes for business-field branches such as Amount >= 5000 and Amount < 5000.
Place multiple gateway branch targets on the same x column with separated y lanes.
Approval Assignment Tasks must expose Approved and Rejected outcomes; Complete Tasks normally expose Completed only.
Use shared End with Rejection nodes for one to three adjacent approval tasks, positioned above or below the source task group center.
Do not force vertices on every rejected or cross-lane line; use vertices only for return/backward lines, overlap avoidance, node crossing, or long reroutes.

required: Every non-SequenceFlow node must carry a top-level position object with numeric x/y coordinates.
Generated nodes must not share identical or near-identical coordinates.
Complex workflows must reserve enough canvas width and height for the number of planned nodes.
Approval Assignment Task nodes must include Approved and Rejected outcome SequenceFlows unless the workflow action is explicitly not an approval task.
Complete Assignment Task nodes must include a Completed outcome SequenceFlow.
Business-field branches must use an InclusiveGateway rather than direct condition fan-out from an Assignment Task.
Rejected, cancel, and exception paths must use a separate lane from the main approval path.
A shared EndRejectEvent should collect at most three adjacent Approval Assignment Task Rejected flows.
A shared EndRejectEvent may only collect Rejected flows from Approval Assignment Tasks on the same horizontal lane. Assignment Tasks on different y lanes must use separate EndRejectEvent nodes.

A shared EndRejectEvent must be centered by node center points, not top-left coordinates. When three Assignment Tasks share one EndRejectEvent, the EndRejectEvent center x must match the center of the three-task span, normally the middle task center.
A shared EndRejectEvent must collect only local adjacent sources; if rejected sources are far apart even on the same lane, split them into separate local EndRejectEvent nodes.

If the source Approval Assignment Tasks are on the first workflow row, their shared EndRejectEvent must be placed on the row above them. If the source tasks are on a lower row, the shared EndRejectEvent must be placed on the row below them.

Workflow diagrams should use the roughly 16:9 Designer canvas area. Large workflows should add vertical rows instead of putting all nodes on one long horizontal line, and generated workflows should not exceed five vertical rows.
Medium-complexity workflows should not stretch into a very wide horizontal strip; fold later branches into upper or lower lanes instead of continuing the main path far to the right.
Complex workflow rows should not become dense control strips; split later steps, branch targets, and post-approval actions into upper/lower lanes before row labels and SequenceFlows overlap.
Long gateway branch labels must not share the same horizontal label lane; when two or more sibling condition labels are long, their target nodes should be separated into distinct y lanes.

Backward or return SequenceFlow edges must use rounded routing and explicit vertices. Standard-spaced forward rejected or cross-lane edges do not require vertices unless they overlap or cross another node.
Direct same-row Start-to-first-task Submitted connectors must not include non-empty vertices.
Direct same-row adjacent task connectors must not include cosmetic vertices when there is no obstacle.
When a connector uses a horizontal route segment between two workflow rows, its route y must be the midpoint of the net row gap: (upperRowY + workflowNodeHeight + lowerRowY) / 2.

When a long return/backward connector crosses more than two workflow rows, its horizontal route segment must choose an adjacent open row-gap midpoint and must not run through any intermediate workflow row node bounds.
When a connector uses a vertical route segment between two workflow columns, its route x must be the midpoint of the net column gap: (leftColumnX + workflowNodeWidth + rightColumnX) / 2.

Long connector vertical segments must not run through any intermediate workflow column node bounds. If the target vertical lane would overlap a node, choose an adjacent column-gap midpoint, widen columns, or use a justified external lane.

Workflow layout is node-placement first and connector-routing second. If many non-return connectors need vertices to become readable, the generated node layout is wrong and must be rebuilt into the golden-reference main lane, branch lane, action lane, and local rejection motifs.

Vertices are last-mile corrections only. They are allowed for backward/return, rejected, overlap, node-crossing, and genuinely long reroute cases, but must not be used as the primary way to make a workflow diagram readable.

Connector detours must stay proportionate to the source-target distance. A connector whose routed path is much longer than the direct Manhattan path indicates poor node placement and must fail generated-final validation.
Use an external return lane only for long backward/return/cross-lane reroutes where midpoint routing is unsafe or crowded.
SequenceFlow source and target references must resolve to existing workflow node ids.
Generated graphposition should cover the visual node bounds plus padding.

lineStyle.rejectedPath: rounded; vertices required only for backward/return, overlap, node crossing, or long reroute
lineStyle.crossLanePath: rounded; vertices required only when spacing cannot prevent overlap

endRejectRules.oneSourcePlacement: align EndRejectEvent center x with the source task center x
endRejectRules.twoOrThreeSourcePlacement: align EndRejectEvent center x near the source task group center by node center points; for three sources this is normally the middle task center
endRejectRules.sharedEndRejectLaneRule: all approval-task rejected sources connected to the same EndRejectEvent must be on the same horizontal lane within endRejectSharedLaneToleranceY
endRejectRules.firstRowPlacement: place shared EndRejectEvent above first-row Approval Assignment Tasks
endRejectRules.lowerRowPlacement: place shared EndRejectEvent below Approval Assignment Tasks that are not on the first workflow row
